fr.elements: Main Building; Vehicle Parking Area; Landscaped Area; Sidewalk Network; Perimeter Fencing
fr.considerations: Multiple Access Points; Limited Rear Access
fr.recommendations: Improving emergency response effectiveness at this property could be achieved by ensuring all access points are clearly marked and free from obstructions to facilitate rapid entry by responders. Enhanced signage indicating multiple access points, as well as clear directions within the vehicle parking area, may assist in expediting navigation and staging. It may also be beneficial to evaluate the sidewalk network to ensure that it remains unobstructed, allowing for efficient movement of personnel and equipment. While perimeter fencing contributes to site security, maintaining adequate openings or access gates at strategic points could support rapid responder access to the building and landscaped areas. Addressing the limited rear access by considering contingency routes or emergency cut-throughs in the fencing could further aid in effective emergency operations on the property.

wildfire.conditions: Dense Tree Canopy; Vegetation Adjacent to Structure; Scattered Shrubs; Open Terrain; Limited Defensible Space
wildfire.recommendations: Enhancing defensible space by increasing the separation between dense tree canopies and structures may significantly reduce wildfire exposure. Managing the vegetation directly adjacent to structures is crucial, as clearing or thinning it could help minimize fuel continuity. Continuous management of scattered shrubs across the property and maintaining open terrain could support long-term resilience by reducing available fuels. Expanding defensible space areas would aid in slowing fire progression towards built improvements and provide necessary clearance for firefighting efforts. Moreover, maintaining existing open spaces and managing vegetation in these zones can improve access for suppression activities while contributing positively to overall property preparedness and wildfire resilience.
